clean_story:
Underground architects. Nighttime symphonists.

The word cricket comes from the Old French criquet, mimicking their sharp, rhythmic chirps—like tiny percussionists tuning the dark.

Crickets thrive where others falter: in cracks of desert rocks, beneath rotting logs, or inside self-dug burrows no wider than a pencil. Their habitats demand two things—moisture to keep their exoskeletons flexible, and warmth to speed their metabolism (below 50 degrees Fahrenheit, they fall silent). Field crickets prefer meadows; cave crickets cling to damp walls, leaping like fleas but blind. The snowy tree cricket, though, stakes its life on a single leaf, chewing a V-shaped notch to drink sap—a survival hack so precise, farmers once used its chirp rate to gauge temperature (count 40 chirps in 13 seconds = 55 degrees Fahrenheit).

Their real estate choices are tactical. Underground tunnels buffer heat; leaf litter hides them from birds. Even urban crickets exploit sidewalk gaps, turning cities into concrete grasslands. Yet all share one rule: avoid open skies. Moonlight makes them targets.

Three related subjects are insect thermoregulation, bioacoustics in mating, and how soil shapes ecosystems.
